skill.name: neon-postgres
skill.description: Guidance for building with Neon serverless Postgres, including Prisma, Drizzle, branching, and PgBouncer connection pooling.
skill.summary: Apply practical Neon Postgres patterns for serverless applications and ORM integrations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged database connection string terminology and two weak cryptography patterns in SKILL.md. Review found documentation-only references to Neon connection URLs and PgBouncer, with no executable code, no credential collection, and no weak cryptographic implementation. No prompt injection attempt or data exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Database URL Terminology Requires Secret Handling Care
low_findings.description: SKILL.md references DATABASE_URL and DIRECT_URL as connection string names for Prisma with Neon. This is legitimate database setup guidance, but users should avoid pasting real credentials into prompts or shared logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines describe expected environment variable names for Neon and Prisma, not code that reads or transmits secrets. The remaining concern is user handling of database credentials.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography at the skill description and PgBouncer guidance. The referenced lines contain product and connection pooling text, not hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain plain documentation text and no code path. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic operations.

content.value_statement: Serverless database setup can fail when pooling, migrations, and ORM drivers use the wrong connection mode. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for Neon Postgres patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use pooled and direct Neon connection strings.
Guides Prisma setups that separate client traffic from migrations.
Compares Drizzle neon-http and neon-serverless driver options.
Summarizes PgBouncer pooling behavior and connection limits.
Highlights Neon branching and serverless Postgres considerations.
limitations: Does not connect to Neon or inspect a live database project.
Does not generate credentials or manage secrets.
Does not replace official Neon, Prisma, or Drizzle documentation.
Contains high-level guidance rather than complete application templates.

use_cases.title: Configure Prisma for Neon
use_cases.description: Separate pooled application queries from direct migration traffic in a serverless Postgres project.

use_cases.title: Choose a Drizzle Driver
use_cases.description: Select the Neon HTTP or WebSocket driver based on query shape, sessions, and transaction needs.

use_cases.title: Review Pooling Assumptions
use_cases.description: Check whether connection pooling choices match expected concurrency and migration workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Neon Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how I should set up Neon Postgres for a serverless application using Prisma.

prompt_templates.title: Review Connection URLs
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Neon connection strategy. I will use pooled connections for app queries and direct connections for migrations.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Drizzle Drivers
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare neon-http and neon-serverless for my Drizzle application. Include when transactions or sessions change the choice.

prompt_templates.title: Design Production Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production Neon Postgres pattern for a high-concurrency serverless app using branching, pooling, migrations, and ORM access.

output_examples.input: I need Prisma with Neon in a serverless app.
output_examples.output: Use a pooled connection for normal Prisma Client queries and a direct connection for migration commands. Keep both values in secret storage.

output_examples.input: Should Drizzle use HTTP or WebSocket with Neon?
output_examples.output: Use the HTTP driver for simple single queries. Use the serverless WebSocket driver when your workflow needs transactions or session behavior.

output_examples.input: Can PgBouncer solve all connection limit issues?
output_examples.output: Pooling helps with high application concurrency, but pooled connections still map to underlying database capacity. Review workload size and migration behavior.

best_practices: Keep pooled and direct connection strings separate in secret management.
Use direct connections for schema migrations and DDL operations.
Choose the Neon driver based on transactions, sessions, and query frequency.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real database credentials into prompts or examples.
Do not run migrations through pooled PgBouncer connections.
Do not assume high pooler limits remove all database capacity limits.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to my Neon database?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance only and does not access external services.

faq.question: Why are two Neon connection strings recommended?
faq.answer: Application queries usually use pooled connections, while migrations need direct connections for DDL operations.

faq.question: Is it safe to share DATABASE_URL with the assistant?
faq.answer: Do not share real database credentials. Use redacted examples when asking for configuration help.

faq.question: Can it help with Prisma?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains Prisma Client and Prisma Migrate connection patterns for Neon.

faq.question: Can it help with Drizzle?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains when to use Neon's HTTP and WebSocket serverless drivers.

faq.question: Does it cover Neon branching?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes high-level guidance for Neon branching in development and deployment workflows.
